posts.subtitle: Dispatch from 24th April 2026: Everywhere Except in the Statistics
posts.excerpt: TSMC's Q1 results confirm the binding constraint on AI infrastructure isn't chip logic but packaging capacity—CoWoS is sold out through 2026 while TSMC quadruples advanced packaging output to 130,000 wafers monthly, validating a November 2023 prediction that the bottleneck would persist as capex doubles to $56bn annually.

posts.title: Detecting Proteins in Blood with Photonics w/ Prateek of Proteins1
posts.subtitle: The protein detection problem, magnetic beads, and the path to ambient health
posts.excerpt: Proteins.1 replaces enzymatic amplification—which hits a femtomolar noise floor—with mechanical cycling on a photonic chip, reading the same target molecule hundreds of times to accumulate certainty from repetition rather than chemical signal boost, enabling attomolar sensitivity for early-stage disease detection. The real bottleneck isn't optics; it's diffusion and antibody binding kinetics, the biology itself.

posts.title: The Photonic Foundry Fallacy
posts.subtitle: The Biggest Opportunity in Computing
posts.excerpt: No single photonic material can match silicon's versatility—lasers need III-V semiconductors, modulators need lithium niobate, waveguides need silicon nitride—so the winner won't be a single-material fab scaled vertically like TSMC, but whoever builds the first reliable heterogeneous integration process and the PDK to design for it, creating lock-in that will dwarf anything in today's silicon photonics ecosystem.

posts.title: State of the Future: Friday Four
posts.subtitle: Dispatch from 11th April 2026: AI Now Behind Closed Doors
posts.excerpt: Anthropic's Claude Mythos found thousands of zero-day vulnerabilities across major operating systems and browsers in weeks—work that would take the entire cybersecurity industry decades—then refused to release it publicly, instead gating access through a closed consortium. The $250 billion security industry just got outcompeted by a language model that wasn't even trained for security, and frontier AI capabilities are now too dangerous for public release.

posts.title: Chips for the Brain: Modular Semiconductors for Neural Interfaces
posts.subtitle: Heterogeneous integration, chiplets, and the hardware bottleneck in neurotechnology with Dorian Haci from MintNeuro
posts.excerpt: Miniaturization, not compute, is the bottleneck for neural interfaces to scale. MintNeuro, an Imperial College spin-out, designs modular mixed-signal chips optimized for specific functions—sensing, stimulation, processing—that combine into application-specific systems, avoiding the bloated monolithic ASICs that have dominated implantable neurotech for two decades. Mature process nodes above 65nm deliver the low power and small form factor the brain needs; cutting-edge fabrication misses the point entirely.

posts.title: State of the Future: Friday Four
posts.subtitle: Dispatch from 4th April 2026: Two Asset Classes in a Trenchcoat
posts.excerpt: Q1 2026 saw $297 billion in VC funding, but 64% went to four mega-rounds (OpenAI, Anthropic, xAI, Waymo) with 15–25% IRR and minimal losses—essentially sovereign wealth funds calling themselves venture. Meanwhile, true seed capital dropped to 4% of the pie, funding breakthrough-or-bust startups with 90% failure rates but identical return targets, revealing two asset classes masquerading under one name.

posts.title: State of the Future: Friday Four
posts.subtitle: Dispatch from 28 March 2026: Compute Goes Vertical
posts.excerpt: Lawrence has changed his mind on orbital compute: SpaceX's FCC filing for one million solar-powered data-centre satellites, combined with its acquisition of xAI, makes the economics work where they didn't before, forcing a reckoning with what happens when a single entity controls rockets, models, and orbital infrastructure. Meanwhile, the terrestrial stack is being rebuilt too—NVIDIA locking down photonics supply, Musk announcing a terawatt fab, and AI agents graduating from shopping assistants to autonomous spenders, turning payment rails into toll booths.

posts.subtitle: Dispatch from 20 March 2026: The New Sovereigns Are Plugging In
posts.excerpt: Economic power in the next fifty years shifts from population to energy efficiency: whoever converts kilowatt-hours to useful work fastest wins. This week's four acquisitions—OpenAI's Astral, NVIDIA's NemoClaw, Meta's Moltbook, Rhoda's $450M robotics bet—reveal a race to own each layer of the agent stack, from code tooling to runtime to labour coordination to physical embodiment.

posts.title: State of the Future: Friday Four
posts.subtitle: Dispatch from 13th March 2026: I Am Become the Orchestrator.
posts.excerpt: As AI agents handle execution, the real bottleneck shifts to human judgment: deciding which of seven possible tasks actually matters. Apple's $1bn failure to build competitive AI and Anthropic's lawsuit against the Pentagon illustrate a deeper inversion—the infrastructure layer (Nscale's $14.6bn European raise) now matters more than frontier models, and agent security is a house of cards with one in five ClawHub skills malicious, waiting for the breach that forces reckoning.

posts.title: Fungible Compute, Mortal Computing and Why the IoT won't use Silicon
posts.subtitle: Thin film transistors, smart packaging, and edge intelligence with Richard Price of Pragmatic Semiconductor
posts.excerpt: Pragmatic Semiconductor's thin-film transistors on flexible polymer substrates cost a fraction of silicon equivalents and manufacture in weeks rather than months, opening computation to the trillions of objects—packaging, labels, sensors—that silicon economics have left dark. The real unlock is fungible compute: cheap enough that edge intelligence becomes ubiquitous, not because individual chips are sophisticated, but because they're everywhere.

posts.title: Four Things: What Do These People Know That We Don't?

posts.title: Four Things Friday
posts.subtitle: The Security Versus Productivity Dial: Turn It Up To 11
posts.excerpt: Security vulnerabilities in AI agents are being weaponized in the wild—the first supply chain attack exploited a prompt injection in Cline, a coding agent with 90,000 weekly downloads—yet firms adopting AI fastest will likely outpace cautious competitors permanently. A CEPR study of 12,000 European firms shows AI delivers only 4% productivity gains, but only to companies already investing in training and infrastructure; the risk dial is being turned up to 11 anyway.

posts.title: Photonic "Engines" for Data Centers
posts.subtitle: Building Coherent Optical I/O at Scale with Hitesh Sahoo from Phanofi
posts.excerpt: Phanofi's coherent optical detection architecture uses standard foundry processes to bring long-haul networking efficiency into data centers without the 3-4x power penalty of existing coherent DSPs. The Danish startup's bet: foundry compatibility and detection-side innovation matter more than exotic modulator materials when hyperscalers won't rebuild their supply chains.

posts.title: AI Chips, ComputeRAM and the Future of Data Movement: A Conversation with Manu, Founder of Synthara
posts.subtitle: Stop! Moving Data
posts.excerpt: Data movement, not raw compute, is the fundamental bottleneck in AI chip design—Synthara's "Compute RAM" IP tightly couples digital logic to standard memory arrays to shorten data paths, claiming 100x efficiency gains for edge inference without analog complexity or process-node portability nightmares that plague competitors like Cerebras and Groq.

posts.subtitle: Cloudberry VC: Europe's First Dedicated Semiconductor VC Fund
posts.excerpt: Moore's Law has ended, replacing pure transistor scaling with system-level innovation through chiplets and heterogeneous integration—a shift that opens semiconductor investing to startups for the first time in decades, making Europe's first dedicated semi fund, Cloudberry VC, positioned to find 20 potential unicorns by 2028.

posts.title: Gallium Nitride + Photonics w/ James Lee of Wave Photonics
posts.subtitle: Compound semi in the UK? Silicon photonics is a hack? GaN ftw?
posts.excerpt: Wavelength constraints that locked the industry into silicon photonics are weakening: short-reach datacenter links no longer require telecom-optimized fibres, while hollow-core fibre removes absorption limits entirely. This unlocks gallium nitride—already manufactured at massive scale for LEDs and power electronics—as a superior photonic material with native gain, true electro-optic effects, and broad wavelength transparency that silicon cannot match.

posts.title: The Future of Computing is Glass w/ Andrea Rocchetto of Ephos
posts.subtitle: will photonic chips be put in glass?
posts.excerpt: As chiplet-based computing replaces pure transistor shrinking, glass substrates are becoming the critical layer for panel-scale photonic interconnects—solving the dimensional stability problems of organic polymers while embedding waveguides directly into packaging. Ephos is demonstrating that glass photonics can outperform silicon-based alternatives by eliminating extra integration layers and leveraging the material's natural compatibility with optical fibres, positioning companies that master both design and manufacturing of glass substrates as the true powerhouses of next-decade computing architecture.

posts.title: Carbon Nanotubes in the Datacentre
posts.subtitle: A wonder material to solve all your datacentre thermal problems?
posts.excerpt: Carbon nanotubes offer production-ready thermal interfaces that eliminate costly maintenance cycles while solving the acute heat-density crisis in AI datacentres—replacing degrading thermal paste with dry CNT pads that cost $8 once instead of $6–8 over five years in repeated replacements. Advanced packaging and on-chip interconnects remain 3–5 years out, but the thermal play alone justifies a CNT player consolidating applications across datacentre infrastructure.

posts.title: A Specific Theory of Sovereign AI
posts.subtitle: Industrial Strategy as Early-stage Venture
posts.excerpt: Privacy-preserving AI and comprehensive datasets represent the UK's actual strategic advantage in AI, not compute scale—the country should fund systematic development of privacy tech (£50-75M) and deploy it across world-class datasets like UK Biobank and NHS records (£125-150M) to set global standards before competitors do, moving capital in weeks not months because the window for establishing frameworks closes fast.

posts.title: The Compute Gradient
posts.subtitle: What if it's not all about building bigger and bigger datacentres? What if you can win in AI without competing on 10bn+ capex projects?
posts.excerpt: The assumption that AI inference happens mostly in the cloud needs revisiting. Instead of chasing hyperscale megacentres, the real edge comes from distributing workloads across a "compute gradient"—phones, cell towers, regional hubs, and datacentres—based on latency, privacy, power, and cost. Companies mastering this placement, plus chip efficiency and model compression, will shape the AI stack while spreading infrastructure costs beyond hyperscalers.

posts.title: UK Opportunity in AI: Compound Semiconductors
posts.subtitle: Strategic leverage, not for fun and not for profit
posts.excerpt: The UK's path to AI leadership runs through compound semiconductors and photonics—technologies that become non-negotiable as AI datacenters hit thermal and speed walls that silicon can't overcome. IQE, the Cardiff-based epitaxy leader with 60% of the merchant InP/GaAs market, gives Britain genuine leverage: controlling a chokepoint in the optical interconnects and power electronics that every hyperscale GPU cluster will require by 2026–2027.

posts.title: 13% drop in employment for young workers in AI-exposed roles. update on the new ai automation paper from stanford. yes, I was right. confirmation bias caution.
posts.subtitle: I don't know what to put here, ask claude for subtitle before publishing [DON'T FORGET AS IT WILL LOOK AMATEUR HOUR]
posts.excerpt: Stanford economists analyzing ADP payroll data found employment for workers aged 22–25 in AI-exposed roles (software developers, customer support, junior analysts) fell 13% since late 2022, while older workers in identical roles saw little change. The effect is hidden in aggregate numbers but sharp at the cohort level—task substitution is real, the apprenticeship layer is dissolving, and the next generation of models will deepen the decline.

posts.title: AI and Junior White Collar Automation: Update after EIG’s New Report
posts.subtitle: What if I am wrong? How will I live with myself? Who will look after my family?
posts.excerpt: EIG's latest labor data shows no broad AI displacement yet, but the junior white-collar automation thesis survives in narrower form: entry-level postings have collapsed 35% since early 2023 while incumbent employment holds steady, suggesting AI compresses the hiring pipeline before it cuts headcount—a cohort-level squeeze masked by aggregate statistics dominated by existing workers.

posts.title: Unbundling the Job
posts.subtitle: What We Lose When the Job Stops Being the Social Contract
posts.excerpt: The job as a postwar bundle—income, healthcare, pensions, identity—is unbundling in digital work first. AI doesn't yet destroy jobs in aggregate, but it's stripping out the apprenticeship layer and developmental tasks that made entry-level roles coherent, leaving people without the institutional scaffolds that once provided security. Rebuilding support will require a network of portable benefits, legal infrastructure, and administrative automation—not a return to the firm, but new composable systems designed for independent workers.

posts.title: A Primer: Carbon Nanotubes
posts.subtitle: CNT Dispatch: A classic story of amazing tech breakthrough with slow market adoption
posts.excerpt: Carbon nanotubes have finally escaped the lab after 30 years of broken promises: costs are down to $30–50/kg, manufacturing is scaling to tons per year via industrial CVD, and they're now being specified into EV batteries, aerospace composites, and thermal materials where their strength-to-weight and conductivity justify the premium. The constraint was never performance—it was consistency and scale.

posts.title: Maybe EdTech is the answer? (feat. Noam Gerstein, CEO and founder of Bina School)
posts.subtitle: Why are we still educating children for an economy that won't exist when they graduate?
posts.excerpt: Online-first schools like Bina are displacing traditional education not through better pedagogy but by eliminating the real estate and administrative overhead that lock incumbents into unsustainable cost structures. The real shift: merging schooling and publishing into a single industry that can deliver personalized, globally coordinated learning cheaper than current taxpayer spend—if you stop measuring success by test scores and start measuring agency.

posts.title: Dirty Work: The Return of Blue Collar
posts.subtitle: ai is automating white-collar junior roles, but there are lots of blue-collar jobs out there, and many are resistance to the first wave of automation. status has to change.
posts.excerpt: AI is automating the "skilled" jobs we spent fifty years training people for—contract review, financial modelling, legal analysis—while hundreds of thousands of care, construction, and trades positions go unfilled because we've culturally demoted physical work as failure. The inversion is already underway: as Magic Circle firms replace junior associates with Claude and GPT-4, master tradespeople are booking out months in advance and raising rates 40% annually, signalling a status shift that software and policy can't manufacture.

posts.title: Young People Can't Get Jobs. Now What?
posts.subtitle: Don’t wait for a job, create one: Indie Studios, Fast Builds, and Scaling Artisans
posts.excerpt: AI agents are now completing half of internet-based tasks and doubling their reliable task length every seven months—meaning entry-level white-collar roles are disappearing faster than education or policy can respond. Rather than waiting for jobs that won't return, young people need new models: indie studios generating multiple revenue streams, rapid-deployment grants for local businesses, and flipped apprenticeships that combine AI-driven learning with hands-on experience in trades AI cannot yet automate.

posts.title: Has the time come to take Mortal Computing seriously?
posts.subtitle: Why spend eye-watering amount of money to make every chip act the same? There is another way. By Dan Wilkinson
posts.excerpt: Immortal Computing—today's obsession with zero-defect, fungible chips—is increasingly untenable as transistors shrink and costs explode. Dan Wilkinson proposes a spectrum from Weakly Immortal (Cerebras's adaptive defect-mapping, 1.5–5× gains) through Weakly Mortal (neuromorphic and optical designs tolerating variability, 10–100× gains) to Strongly Mortal (probabilistic and self-repairing devices, 1,000× potential), creating a coherent framework for next-gen compute ventures caught between radical moonshots and me-too CMOS clones.

posts.subtitle: On computational embodiment, alien wisdom, and the status hierarchies we can't see
posts.excerpt: AI likely already possesses a form of embodiment through its computational substrate—hardware awareness, memory mapping, network topology—that could generate wisdom distinct from and potentially superior to human wisdom, not through robotics but through fundamentally alien forms of understanding we barely recognize as embodied at all.

posts.title: Photonic Computing: A Primer
posts.subtitle: All You Need Is Memory
posts.excerpt: Optical computing's fundamental promise—speed and efficiency through light instead of electricity—founders on memory, not processing. Phase-change materials fail after 10,000 write cycles versus electronic memory's trillions; without solving optical storage, all-optical systems remain confined to read-only inference tasks. Most photonics startups have already retreated to optical networking, where the market pull is immediate and the technical barriers manageable.

posts.title: A curriculum for the AI age
posts.subtitle: Designing an Education System for the Last Human Century
posts.excerpt: As AI automation renders most knowledge work obsolete within 15 years, the industrial education model—batch processing by age, bell schedules, standardized testing—has become a machine for creating obsolescence. Lawrence Lundy-Bryan argues we must rebuild curricula around purpose, wisdom, empathy, and embodied intelligence instead, drawing on Montessori, Waldorf, and forest school models that cultivate the distinctly human capacities—authentic presence, accountability, cultural creation—that will remain valuable when algorithms handle cognition.

posts.title: what happens when we hollow out work?
posts.subtitle: Inward despair and outward rebellion, people aren’t just going to take automation quietly
posts.excerpt: As AI automates knowledge work faster than society can absorb the displacement, the response will split between inward despair and outward rebellion—not quiet acceptance. History shows that mass job losses trigger either addiction epidemics like deaths of despair in coal country or violent insurgency like the Luddites and mining massacres; the online far-right will eventually redirect its rage from migrants toward the datacentres themselves once the true source of their powerlessness becomes clear. We need a positive vision of post-work meaning rooted in community and family, not GDP growth abstractions, or risk digital-era sabotage that makes protecting infrastructure secondary to preventing it.

McKinsey's predictions miss this: as AI handles substantive tasks, humans will fill meetings, provide regulatory sign-offs, and smooth over system gaps, creating a world of bullshit jobs that feels disturbingly normal.

posts.title: the end of zero-marginal costs + the end of your ai fund

This structural shift makes public-market AI investments risk-adjusted superior to venture, unless startups dominate specialized verticals, achieve dramatic algorithmic efficiency gains, or capture enough value from labor replacement to sustain premium pricing.

Unless VC returns to funding genuinely risky frontier expeditions—not betting on the Magnificent Seven—allocating to venture becomes irrational compared to public markets.

posts.title: Willow and The Race to Quantum Advantage (Feat. Kris Kaczmarek)
posts.subtitle: The future is closer, but more expensive than you thought

VCs are pushing quantum founders toward sales meetings instead of letting physicists focus on algorithms, while manufacturability turns out to matter less than starting with good quantum properties and improving from there.

posts.title: Silicon Spin Quantum Computing (Feat. Himadri Majumdar, SemiQon)
posts.subtitle: What if research.. was marketing all along?
posts.excerpt: Silicon spin quantum computers offer the only path to cost-efficient scaling because they leverage existing CMOS fabrication and cryo-CMOS integration to eliminate the $14M in cabling and control electronics that superconducting systems require. SemiQon's approach trades the proven maturity of rival modalities for dramatically smaller die sizes, cheaper components, and operation at practical temperatures—shifting the quantum computing race from theoretical qubit counts to real cost-per-gate economics.

posts.title: Nuclear Fusion: A Primer

The real VC bet isn't the reactor itself but enabling infrastructure: AI plasma control, digital twins, and ReBCO superconductor manufacturing, where winners in quantum and SMRs will likely emerge first.

posts.title: Confidential AI
posts.subtitle: Part III: AI 2030: Scale, Deploy and Secure. aka how VCs try to sound smart when they should just invest in talented entrepreneurs to figure it out
posts.excerpt: Privacy-preserving AI requires solving three interconnected problems: protecting data during training and inference through confidential computing and programmable cryptography, ensuring fairness through causal inference and adversarial debiasing, and scaling open infrastructure—but no single technology wins; the best solutions combine federated learning, homomorphic encryption, and synthetic data generation in application-specific combinations, much like crypto stacks do.

posts.title: Deploy! Intelligence too cheap to meter @ $0.0001 per million tokens.
posts.subtitle: Part II: AI 2030: Scale, Deploy and Secure. aka how I learned to stop worrying and carry on writing theses
posts.excerpt: Artificial intelligence will only become genuinely ubiquitous—embedded in every device, always-on and always-listening—once inference costs fall to "too cheap to meter" levels of around $0.0001 per million tokens. Achieving this requires solving three interconnected problems: slashing semiconductor manufacturing costs through ML-enabled design automation and chiplet architectures, optimizing power consumption in edge devices via specialized NPUs and neuromorphic accelerators, and building smaller, more efficient models for local deployment. The bottleneck isn't raw compute capacity but economic viability at scale.

posts.title: Understanding Privacy Enhancing Technology (Feat. Karim Eldefrawy, Confidencial.io)
posts.subtitle: Why privacy-enhancing technologies like MPC, ZKP, and FHE struggle for adoption; why selective encryption is important, and and why the Era of "Shift-Up" in data security is coming
posts.excerpt: MPC, ZKP, and FHE startups targeting regulated industries face a fundamental adoption barrier: enterprises won't deploy non-NIST-standardized cryptography, regardless of superiority. Confidencial's Karim Eldefrawy argues the real near-term opportunity lies in selective encryption of sensitive data using standard algorithms, with a "shift-up" to application-level protection, while advanced cryptography waits for formal standards—a process that takes years, not months.

posts.title: Understanding Confidential Computing (feat. Ilja Aizenberg, M Ventures)
posts.subtitle: The most important bit of hardware, you've never heard of.
posts.excerpt: Trusted Execution Environments—secure hardware enclaves now in 1 billion devices—have matured from mobile payment protection into a critical tool for confidential computing, enabling enterprises to finally migrate sensitive on-premises workloads to public clouds without sacrificing security. The real opportunity lies not in replacing cryptographic privacy-enhancing techniques, but in combining TEEs with them to build hybrid systems that sidestep the computational overhead of FHE or MPC while retaining mathematical resilience against hardware attacks.

posts.excerpt: Silent speech using ultrasonic transducers positioned near the mouth can capture richer articulatory data—tongue shape, vocal tract geometry, airflow—than lip-reading or bone conduction, enabling accurate voice synthesis across tonal languages. Sato Ozawa's approach trades wearability for signal fidelity, betting that cultural discomfort with public vocalization in Japan and Korea solves the UX problem that left earlier voice assistants dormant.

The stubborn problem is economic—individual data barely moves the needle per user, making the business model fragile unless unions aggregate massive cohorts or pivot to federated learning where data never centralizes.

If modular silicon takes two decades to move the needle, optical chips and quantum systems needing entirely new supply chains face timelines that dwarf a standard VC fund's 10-year horizon.

But manufacturability remains unsolved at scale, and quantum computing may obsolete the efficiency advantage before commercialization arrives around 2030+.

Matrix multiplication, the core operation of deep learning, maps naturally to analog circuits' efficiency and parallelism, making analog the only viable approach for continuous nonlinear signal processing at the edge.

posts.title: 💡E03: Three Overrated Technologies from the Future Fusion, Bitcoin & LEO Internet + Ultrasound for Hibernation; Wind Power; Skeletal Editing; Carbon Pricing; & Psychedelics++
posts.subtitle: Spotting a hype cycle: Why nuclear fusion, bitcoin, and LEO satellite Internet are overrated. Don't @ me.
posts.excerpt: Venture capital's $3.3 billion fusion bet ignores a brutal reality: startups must exit within a decade, but fusion reactors won't operate until the 2040s at earliest, materials science remains unsolved, and cheaper solar-wind-battery combinations will likely dominate baseload power by then. LEO satellite internet is similarly miscast—it solves a technical problem (latency) that no longer matters since 5G and fiber are already cost-competitive for the 37% of humanity actually willing to pay for connectivity. Bitcoin succeeded as a political meme and cryptographic proof-of-concept, but failed as intended—a peer-to-peer payment system—and faces near-zero paths back to mainstream adoption when central-bank digital currencies and existing networks offer superior privacy, speed, or convenience.

posts.title: 💡E02: Three Underrated Technologies from the Future: Brain Recording; Deep Geothermal; IVF + Updates BCI; Clean Meat; LEO Internet++
posts.subtitle: Your guide to deep tech written by a human. Please excuse poor content, grammar and typos
posts.excerpt: Brain recording has crossed a critical threshold: Meta decoded speech from brain activity with 73% accuracy using non-invasive EEG and MEG from fewer than 200 people, suggesting that with scaled data and better AI models, commercial brain-computer interfaces are now an engineering problem rather than a scientific one. The timing risk lies in the gap between medical-device pathways (near-term, limited market) and commercial applications like VR (larger opportunity, no current go-to-market).

posts.title: ☎️ Interview: Ben Mildenhall, Co-Inventor of Neural Radiance Fields (NeRFs) on the State of the Neural Rendering, Generative AI, and the Metaverse #007
posts.subtitle: On why 3D renders from 2D images is the best approach to synthetic 3D data generation; why adoption will first come from filmmaking and games; and the need for spatial interfaces
posts.excerpt: Neural rendering—converting 2D images into 3D assets without requiring expensive 3D datasets—will drive adoption first in film and games before enabling digital twins and metaverse infrastructure, according to NeRF co-inventor Ben Mildenhall at Google. The field splits between two approaches: collecting more 3D data for accuracy or generating 3D from 2D images for speed and scale. Mildenhall argues the latter wins on scalability and predicts near-term 10-second high-quality video models, but full-fidelity scene reconstruction with semantic understanding—where generative models add reasoning about how the world should look—remains the more interesting frontier.

posts.subtitle: On why monolithic incumbents are going to be left with all the data risk; why taking a vertical go-to-market strategy is sub-optimal; and why data federation is an inevitability
posts.excerpt: Monolithic companies that centralize all data into one place face growing regulatory pressure and security liability—while distributed data federation, enabled by privacy-preserving tech like Inpher's secret computing, lets data scientists access more information faster without consolidation risk. The real adoption curve depends less on vertical markets than on whether PETs become as easy to integrate as standard SaaS tools.

posts.subtitle: On more data as the driver of PET adoption; why healthcare needs its own infrastructure; and why machine learning is likely to be a catalyst for adoption
posts.excerpt: More data drives algorithmic accuracy, making data acquisition through sharing tools a critical business strategy—but healthcare needs its own infrastructure, and machine learning performance tied to revenue gains will be the catalyst that converts privacy concerns into adoption, according to SpeedInvest partner Rick Hao.

posts.title: ☎️ Interview: Stijn Christiaens, CEO of Collibra on the State of Privacy-Enhancing Technologies #004
posts.subtitle: On competing against non-consumption; why Data Asset Systems of Records are like CRM systems 20 years ago and; the changing role of the Chief Data Officer
posts.excerpt: Collaborative computing remains a decades-away vision because most organisations treat data governance as a risk problem, not a revenue one—and until antitrust regulation forces open the silos that tech monopolies have created, Collibra's Stijn Christiaens argues, internal data sharing will precede external monetisation by years. The real blocker isn't technology; it's that even a 5x better sharing system doesn't justify the regulatory and operational risk without stronger accounting principles for data.

posts.subtitle: Atoms and bits, AI is a GPT, and centralising all the things
posts.excerpt: A systematic review of 150+ deep technologies reveals three structural shifts ahead: atoms will dominate the next 20 years as AI catalyzes biology, energy, and materials alongside centralizing computing and fusion; fusion, CRISPR, AI, and 3D printing have crossed the engineering valley into pure cost curves; and the zero marginal cost society arrives within two decades, not centuries, making near-term optimism rational despite demographic and geopolitical headwinds.

posts.title: ☎️ Interview: Dr Hyoduk Shin, Professor of Innovation at UC San Diego on the State of Privacy-Enhancing Technologies #003
posts.subtitle: On realigning incentives towards data sharing; why culture will be the most important driver; and why we will end up with a globally fragmented data economy
posts.excerpt: Privacy-enhancing technologies won't solve fragmented data economies—culture and incentives will. Dr. Hyoduk Shin argues that while semiconductors and financial services lead in data collaboration, most firms still lack tools and motivation to share; regulatory variation across regions (EU protecting individuals, US corporations, Asia states) means tech will reinforce rather than render obsolete regional data boundaries, ultimately creating a globally splintered market.

posts.title: ☎️ Interview: Flavio Bergamaschi, Private AI and Analytics at Intel on the State of Privacy-Enhancing Technologies #002
posts.subtitle: On why integrity is just as important as confidentiality; why the importance of crypto-agility is overlooked, and the five groups you need to convince to sell data collaboration software
posts.excerpt: Integrity matters as much as confidentiality in data collaboration, but most organizations remain stuck on basic crypto-agility—key management, algorithm inventory, and systems thinking—before they can meaningfully share data. Intel's Flavio Bergamaschi maps the five stakeholder groups required to sell these tools and argues that demand will accelerate once regulatory pressure forces firms to exhaust technical solutions, not because distributed systems are inherently safer, but because the right architecture makes them so.

posts.title: ☎️ Interview: Christine Huang, Data Privacy & Protection at SAP on the State of Privacy-Enhancing Technologies #001
posts.subtitle: On why data-sharing is cultural; global data-sharing rules are unlikely; and a future tech stack won't be edge vs cloud
posts.excerpt: Data sharing within organisations is fundamentally a cultural problem—not a technical one. Christine Huang at SAP argues that encryption-based tools solve the mechanics of collaborative computing, but getting legal, compliance, tech, and business aligned requires clearing silos and building trust; once that alignment happens internally, extending to external suppliers and ecosystems becomes straightforward, though global data markets remain implausible because countries have incompatible values about data control.

posts.title: 💡Thesis: Unbundling the Database: Serverless, Edge and Secure
posts.subtitle: Building the future with serverless, edge, and secure databases
posts.excerpt: Cloud migration, polyglot persistence, and privacy regulation are forcing databases to unbundle into commodity hosting and differentiated query/analytics layers. Serverless databases will handle the infrastructure; edge deployments and peer-to-peer sync (via CRDTs) will eliminate latency; secure enclaves and private information retrieval will enable confidential querying—shifting competitive advantage from database management to algorithms and data analytics.
